channel.title: The Grant Desk - US, next 90 days
channel.description: Paid open calls, fellowships and residencies in AI, tech, research, and digital and mixed-media arts, sorted into the right pile. Updated as new calls land on the desk. Filtered to: US, next 90 days.

item.title: Loghaven Artist Residency
item.description:
Organisation: Loghaven Artist Residency

Location: Knoxville, Tennessee, USA

Award: USD 850 weekly living stipend, travel subsidy USD 400 to 800, up to USD 200 materials shipping reimbursement, plus room and board, housing in historic log cabins, and studio space USD 25 non-refundable application fee (fee waiver available on request)

Duration: 2 to 6 weeks depending on session

Deadline: 15 Jul 2026

Loghaven Artist Residency offers practicing artists time, space, and resources on ninety wooded acres in Knoxville, Tennessee. Fellows stay in five rehabilitated historic log cabins with access to purpose-built studio space. Each Fellow receives an USD 850 weekly living stipend, a travel subsidy on a sliding scale from USD 400 to 800, up to USD 200 in materials shipping reimbursement, and covered room and board. Residencies run from 2 to 6 weeks across sessions scheduled throughout 2027. The program is open to artists at any career stage working in architecture, dance, interdisciplinary art, music composition, theater, visual art, and writing, including collaborative groups of up to nine people. Applicants must be at least 21, live more than 120 miles from Knoxville, and be eligible to work legally in the US; international artists and degree-seeking students are not eligible.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: The Center for Fiction / Susan Kamil Emerging Writer Fellowship
item.description:
Organisation: The Center for Fiction

Location: New York City, USA

Award: USD 5,000 grant plus writers studio space, mentorship, agent introductions, and community programming

Duration: One year (the fellowship year)

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

The Center for Fiction / Susan Kamil Emerging Writer Fellowship annually supports nine early-career, New York City-based fiction writers at a pivotal point in their careers. Each fellow receives a 5,000 USD grant, dedicated space and time to write in the Writers Studio, personalized editorial mentorship, opportunities to meet literary agents, and community programming. Applicants must be current residents of one of the five boroughs and must remain in New York City for the entire fellowship year. An emerging writer is defined as someone who has not yet had a novel or short story collection published by a major or independent publisher and who is not currently under contract for a work of fiction; students enrolled in a degree-granting program during the fellowship year are not eligible. The 2027 fellowship application window opens July 1, 2026 and closes July 31, 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: MSB Interpretive Panels: Public Art Commission (Creative Philadelphia)
item.description:
Organisation: Creative Philadelphia (Office of Arts, Culture and the Creative Economy)

Location: Municipal Services Building, Center City, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, United States. Philadelphia-based artists strongly encouraged; artists from outside the region considered. Multiple in-person site visits required (travel costs covered within the project budget).

Award: Total project budget of USD 250,000, which must cover: artist fees (18% design fee), design and engineering, fabrication and installation, structural footings/anchoring, travel and site visits, AR development (software, hosting, content production), insurance (1%), community engagement participation, and a service warranty keeping content compatible with new mobile OS versions for a minimum of 10 years. No application fees.

Duration: Permanent public artwork commission; two-part selection process.

Deadline: 12 Jul 2026

Creative Philadelphia announces a call to commission a series of permanent interpretive panels with integrated augmented reality (AR) components at the Municipal Services Building in Center City Philadelphia. The commission is part of a broader initiative that includes an upcoming public sculpture of Dr. Sadie T.M. Alexander by artist Vinnie Bagwell. Inspired by Alexander's groundbreaking legacy in public service, these interpretive panels will celebrate the City of Philadelphia's municipal employees (past, present, and future) and provide deeper context for understanding Dr. Alexander's impact; they will be located in front of the building's landscaped areas, flanking the upcoming sculpture, and are intended to complement and support the overall site experience. SCOPE: an artist team will design and fabricate four (4) permanent, sculptural interpretive panels incorporating augmented reality (AR) elements to expand storytelling beyond the physical surface of the artwork. ELIGIBILITY: all interested artists are invited to apply; Philadelphia-based artists are strongly encouraged, though artists from outside the region will be considered; multiple in-person site visits are required and associated travel costs must be covered within the project budget. SELECTION: a two-part process: Part 1 submission of qualifications; Part 2 finalist proposals and interviews. Up to three (3) finalists are invited to submit short concept proposals and attend an interview; at no point should artists submit design proposals. BUDGET: USD 250,000 total (see amount for the required cost breakdown). An Artist Informational Meeting is held via Zoom on 30 June, 6:00-7:00pm. Application questions: paige.phillips@phila.gov. Apply via Submittable. Deadline 12 July 2026 (submissions close Monday 13 July 2026, 6:00 AM).

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: GovAI U.S. AI Policy Program
item.description:
Organisation: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)

Location: Washington, D.C., USA (hybrid; remote participation available)

Award: Free to participate; in-person attendees provided dinner (value kept at or below USD 20 per session)

Duration: 12-week part-time program, early September to early December 2026 (approximately 5 hours per week)

Deadline: 05 Jul 2026

The GovAI U.S. AI Policy Program is a bipartisan, part-time, 12-week program designed to accelerate or launch careers in U.S. AI policy. Open to U.S. policy professionals across government and think tanks, it builds a technically-informed understanding of the AI policy landscape through guided discussion sessions, with an emphasis on general-purpose AI. Topics include how general-purpose AI systems are developed; inputs to AI development such as energy, chips, and data centers; AI's effects on productivity, scientific discovery, and economic growth; national security and great power competition; corporate self-governance; AI regulation in other jurisdictions; and federal and state AI regulatory proposals in the U.S. Participants also have weekly opportunities to question world-leading experts; prior guest speakers have included White House OSTP staffers, former frontier-lab employees, and people who helped draft U.S. state- and federal-level AI policy proposals. The program runs early September to early December 2026 (break for Thanksgiving week) with a roughly 5-hour weekly commitment: two hours of synchronous evening sessions (EST), held in-person at GovAI's D.C. office near DuPont Circle or remotely with cameras on, plus three hours of independent reading and podcasts from a free syllabus. To list completion on a resume, participants must attend 9 of 12 sessions, write a short policy memo or op-ed, and pass an interview-based exam. Eligible participants include executive and legislative branch staff (fellows, interns, detailees, military personnel, national laboratory personnel) and think-tank policy researchers and affiliates, across a range of seniority levels, with preference for the D.C. metropolitan area; participants need not currently cover AI but should aspire to a career in U.S. tech or national security policy. Selection has two stages: an online application with six biographical questions, a resume, two verbal reasoning questions, and an approximately 7-minute video; followed by a 30-minute interview in July, with final decisions in early August.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Technology Fellow: Digital Safety and Security
item.description:
Organisation: Ford Foundation

Location: New York, New York, USA

Award: USD 166,000 to 170,000 annual salary, plus comprehensive benefits and retirement contributions of up to 13%

Duration: Two-year term-limited contract

Deadline: 06 Jul 2026

The Ford Foundation seeks a two-year Technology Fellow for Digital Safety and Security to expand the foundation's ability to support grantee digital safety and security. Civil society organizations face targeted information security threats yet many lack the resources and training to maintain robust digital security. Reporting to the Director, Office of the Vice President for U.S. Programs, the Fellow will identify field-building opportunities in the public-interest digital safety and security field, work directly with program officers across US and global teams to integrate digital security capacity into Ford's technical assistance programs, provide strategic support to high-risk grantees, and assess strategic gaps in the ecosystem for safety and security providers. The Technology Fellows Program is administered by Ford's Technology and Society Program and is designed to increase sociotechnical capacity within the Foundation. Requirements include a minimum of 7 years' professional experience with media, technology, advocacy or policy organizations; a baccalaureate degree or equivalent experience; sophisticated knowledge of existing and emerging technologies with a focus on surveillance technologies and organizational digital safety and security; and experience designing, implementing or supporting digital safety and security solutions in a complex global setting. Hybrid role based in New York (three days per week in office) with approximately 10 to 20 percent domestic and international travel. Not eligible for visa sponsorship; candidates must be authorized to work in the United States. Submit both a resume and a cover letter to be considered.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Technology Fellow: AI and Philanthropy
item.description:
Organisation: Ford Foundation

Location: New York, New York, USA

Award: USD 166,000 to 170,000 annual salary, plus comprehensive benefits and retirement contributions of up to 13%

Duration: Two-year term-limited contract

Deadline: 06 Jul 2026

The Ford Foundation seeks a two-year Technology Fellow for AI and Philanthropy, an internally-facing role focused on cross-programmatic learning and advisory support. Reporting to the Director, Technology and Society, the AI Tech Fellow will support Foundation staff in building and adapting internal frameworks for evaluating AI use in ways that prioritize mission and strategy and address ethical, sociotechnical, and operational considerations. Responsibilities include designing and curating hands-on trainings, workshops, and learning sessions to help staff build practical familiarity with AI tools and critically assess their value and efficacy; creating accessible documentation and guides on mission-aligned AI experimentation; curating sociotechnical conversations on how philanthropy and the nonprofit sector are experimenting with AI in ways that respect privacy, safety, and equity; monitoring emerging global AI developments and providing timely briefings to program teams; conducting landscape research on AI initiatives in the philanthropy and social justice sectors; and building relationships with AI researchers, practitioners, grantees and partners working on mission-enabling AI. The Technology Fellows Program is administered by Ford's Technology and Society Program and is designed to increase sociotechnical capacity within the Foundation. Requirements include a minimum of 7 years working within public-interest, government, civil society, or commercial technology sectors; a baccalaureate degree or equivalent experience; demonstrated sociotechnical expertise in artificial intelligence and related technologies including machine learning systems and generative AI and their societal implications; and experience designing, implementing or supporting AI applications and tools in a complex global setting. Hybrid role based in New York (three days per week in office). Not eligible for visa sponsorship; candidates must be authorized to work in the United States. Submit both a resume and a cover letter to be considered.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Wassaic Project 2027 Residency Open Call
item.description:
Organisation: Wassaic Project

Location: Wassaic, New York, USA

Award: Studio space and a private bedroom in a shared house provided; the organization covers approximately USD 5,000 per person through donor and grant support. Residency fee on a sliding scale of USD 0 to 900 per session. USD 25 application fee (waivers available for financial hardship)

Duration: Sessions range from roughly 2 to 4 weeks within the Winter (Jan-April 2027) or Summer (June-Sept 2027) programs

Deadline: 01 Jul 2026

The Wassaic Project runs a single 2027 Artist Residency open call covering both the Winter Residency (January to April 2027) and the Summer Residency (June to September 2027) in Wassaic, New York. Residents receive a private studio (about 100 square feet in winter, 200 to 300 square feet in summer), a private bedroom in a shared house with common spaces and kitchen, and access to a wood shop in both seasons and a print shop in summer. Programming includes open studios, group studio visits, and individual artist talks. The residency operates on a sliding scale fee of USD 0 to 900 per session, with the organization covering roughly USD 5,000 per person through donor and grant support. The open call is open to individual artists, collaborative teams, groups of two or more, and applicants through the Family Residency program. This is the residency program, distinct from the separate Summer Exhibition open call.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Art Shanty Projects 2027: Call for Performances + Art Actions
item.description:
Organisation: Art Shanty Projects

Location: Bde Uman (Lake Harriet), Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States. On-ice program plus several mandatory in-person meetings in Minneapolis; in-person participation required.

Award: Sliding-scale stipend up to USD 1,000 (request an amount that fits the project: roughly USD 1,000 for full-day/most-ambitious activities, USD 500 for half-day, USD 250 for short or one-off/solo/low-production activities). Plus direct production support, discounted materials and resource sharing, a shared warming shanty, a hot meal, snacks, hot beverages, hand/toe warmers, one parking space per project, on-site medic, and ASL interpreter/audio describer for the public. No application fees.

Duration: On-ice program every Saturday and Sunday, 10am-4pm, 16 January to 7 February 2027, with production meetings and mandatory check-ins from September 2026 onward.

Deadline: 09 Jul 2026

Art Shanty Projects seeks winter enthusiasts and creative adventurers to create performances and art actions for its 2027 Program on Bde Uman (Lake Harriet) in Minneapolis. It invites music-makers, poets, builders, scientists, dancers, puppeteers, writers, dreamers, activists, outdoors-people, conversationalists, craftspeople, storytellers, and more to propose new and innovative ways to engage artistically with the public in the social context of a village on a frozen lake (or winter 'beach', as climate change may necessitate). ASP has an expansive notion of what constitutes a performance or art action, having welcomed dance, creative sporting events, happenings, carnival games, music, roving dance, temporary sculpture, puppet shows, artful haircuts, advice booths, climate actions, and more. NOTE: this call is for PERFORMANCES + ART ACTIONS only (a separate call exists for shanty artists). Since 2004, Art Shanty Projects has created joyful winter art experiences inspired by pop-up ice fishing villages, using the frozen lake as a public platform for a temporary creative community with tens of thousands of annual visitors. ASP is deeply committed to social, racial, gender, and economic justice and to accessibility (embracing the spirit of the ADA). ELIGIBILITY: everyone is eligible (artists at all career stages); applicants need not have experience with everything asked, but should show eagerness to engage, develop new skills, and stay open to new perspectives. Projects that involve selling anything or the exchange of legal currency will not be accepted. COMPENSATION: sliding-scale stipend up to USD 1,000 plus extensive production and logistical support. PROGRAM: on-ice every Saturday and Sunday, 10am-4pm, 16 January to 7 February 2027; selected artists notified mid-August 2026, with mandatory in-person meetings (at least one team member) starting 10 September 2026. Apply via the Google form (preferred) or the no-Google-account alternative. Contact Erin Lavelle, Artistic Director: program@artshantyprojects.org. Deadline for proposal submissions: 9 July 2026, 11:59pm CST.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Justice + Joy Summer Research Fellowship 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Justice + Joy

Location: United States; fully virtual program. Capstone showcase tentatively in person (mid-September 2026), with travel and lodging covered.

Award: USD 2,000 stipend paid upon successful program completion, plus skills-based training, guest speaker sessions, and peer community. All travel and lodging for the in-person capstone are covered.

Duration: Seven-week virtual program, 9 July to 20 August 2026, with a capstone showcase mid-September 2026. Weekly Wednesday sessions 5:00-6:30 PM ET plus approximately 2-4 hours of independent work per week.

Deadline: 09 Jul 2026

The Justice + Joy Research Fellowship is a seven-week virtual summer program for girls, young women, and gender-expansive young people of color (ages 18-26) in the United States who want to explore issues impacting their lives, communities, and futures. Fellows identify a topic they care about, such as mental health, education, reproductive justice, immigration, AI and technology, gender-based violence, beauty standards, young parenthood, or another issue affecting their communities, and develop an original research project grounded in their experiences, questions, and ideas. Throughout the summer, fellows participate in weekly virtual sessions, engage with guest speakers, build community with peers from across the country, and receive mentorship and support as they develop their research. The fellowship culminates in a capstone showcase where fellows present their work to the Justice + Joy community, including organizational leaders, partners, and supporters. No prior research experience is required; the program seeks young people who are curious, committed to learning, and interested in using research as a tool for understanding, advocacy, and change. COMMITMENT: weekly virtual sessions on Wednesdays 5:00-6:30 PM ET (9 July - 20 August 2026); approximately 2-4 hours of independent work per week; required orientation on Wednesday 9 July; development of an individual research proposal or conceptual framework; presentation at the capstone showcase (tentatively in person, mid-September 2026, with all travel and lodging covered). WHAT YOU GET: a USD 2,000 stipend paid upon successful completion; skills-based training in research methods, social justice frameworks, and community advocacy; guest speaker sessions; a peer community; and a real audience for the research (the Justice + Joy board and funders). Apply via the Google Form. Note: an explicit application deadline is not stated; the required orientation and program start is 9 July 2026, so apply before then.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: imprint: a QTBIPOC Dance Writing Fellowship
item.description:
Organisation: pateldanceworks

Location: San Francisco Bay Area, California, United States

Award: USD 500 stipend per fellow, plus writing and publishing mentorship

Duration: One program year (approximately September to September)

Deadline: 30 Jun 2026

imprint is an annual dance writing fellowship from pateldanceworks for emerging QTBIPOC (queer, trans, Black, Indigenous, and people of color) dance writers based in the San Francisco Bay Area. Artistic Director Bhumi B Patel supports three selected fellows with writing and publishing mentorship and professional development over a program year. Each fellow receives a USD 500 stipend and commits roughly 30 hours across six cohort meetings that include readings, discussions, writing feedback, and guest speakers. Fellows attend at least five Bay Area dance performances and have two to three completed pieces published on the pateldanceworks blog. The program is not intended for full-time students given the time requirements. There is no application fee.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: PLAYA Art/Sci Awarded Residency 2027
item.description:
Organisation: PLAYA

Location: Summer Lake, Lake County, Oregon, USA

Award: Awarded residency providing free lodging in a private cabin with kitchen and bathroom plus one weekly group meal. Residents cover their own travel, groceries, and materials. No cash stipend. USD 40 application fee (waivers granted on request by June 28, 2026)

Duration: 11 to 25 nights depending on session

Deadline: 30 Jun 2026

PLAYA's Art/Sci Awarded Residency brings together artists and scientists for immersive, interdisciplinary inquiry at a rural retreat near Summer Lake in Lake County, Oregon. The 2027 cycle offers four session options ranging from 11 to 25 nights between January and November 2027. Awarded residents receive free lodging in a private cabin with kitchen and bathroom plus one weekly group meal, while covering their own travel, food, and materials. Both established and emerging practitioners in any media may apply, including naturalists, biologists, sound artists, new media artists, writers, performers, and interdisciplinary creators, and all applicants must be at least 18 years old. Applications are submitted through SlideRoom and require work samples and two references. There is a USD 40 application fee, though PLAYA states all fee waiver requests will be granted if submitted by June 28, 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Windgate Artist-in-Residence Fall 2026 (Arkansas Tech University)
item.description:
Organisation: Arkansas Tech University (supported by the Windgate Charitable Foundation)

Location: Russellville, Arkansas, United States (Russellville campus, in the Arkansas River Valley, about one hour from Little Rock). In-person, full-time residency.

Award: USD 30,000 stipend (minus fringes) plus USD 18,000 for supplies. Housing provided (a 2-bed, 2-bath campus apartment) and a large, climate-controlled, dedicated off-campus studio space.

Duration: Five-month residency over the Fall academic term 2026.

Deadline: 03 Jul 2026

The Artist-in-Residence (AIR) program at Arkansas Tech University is an immersive experience within an art and educational community, intended to advance the careers of practicing artists, present residents as role models for the ATU community, and strengthen public awareness of the significance of making art and its effect on the surrounding community. The residency takes place over a five-month period, giving the artist time and space to focus on the creative process. For Fall 2026, the program will select an artist to create a public artwork that enhances the ATU campus; both two-dimensional and three-dimensional proposals will be considered. The program is supported by a generous grant from the Windgate Charitable Foundation. The Department of Art at ATU offers programs in Fine Art, Graphic Design, Game and Interactive Media Design, and Art Education, with a facility in Norman Hall featuring a professional gallery, computer lab, lecture hall, and studios for painting, drawing, printmaking, sculpture, ceramics, and graphic design. RESIDENCY DETAILS & EXPECTATIONS: residents must be artists with significant exhibition records, strong communication skills, sustained studio practice, and a desire to engage with the larger campus community; involvement may include guest lecturing, public talks, mentoring students, open studios, and/or collaborating with faculty and students. The resident sometimes teaches one course in 2D Design, 3D Design, or Introduction to Drawing during their stay when enrollment allows. Artists are expected to commit as a full-time resident for the Fall academic term 2026. BENEFITS: USD 30,000 stipend (minus fringes), USD 18,000 for supplies, a 2-bed/2-bath campus apartment, and a large climate-controlled off-campus studio. Deadline 3 July 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

The Letters to Strangers Mental Health Scholarship supports students and mental health advocates passionate about mental health awareness, advocacy, and creating positive change in their communities. The award is worth up to USD 8,000 toward education. Apply via the Google Form linked below. Deadline 30 June 2026.

item.title: Fulbright-Iméra Mediterranean Studies Award 2027-2028 (Aix-Marseille, US Faculty)
item.description:
Organisation: Iméra – Aix Marseille Université, in partnership with the Fulbright Commission

Location: Aix-Marseille, France (campus residency)

Award: €2,400 (~USD 2,600) monthly stipend; fully covered accommodation (valued USD 788–1,966/month); €1,200 (~USD 1,300) travel allowance. No application fee.

Duration: 5 or 10 months (September 2027 – June 2028, flexible start/end dates).

Deadline: 15 Sep 2026

The Fulbright-Iméra Mediterranean Studies Award supports US faculty to conduct research at the Iméra Institute for Advanced Study at Aix Marseille Université. ELIGIBILITY: mid-career, senior, or community college faculty affiliated with a US institution of higher education; US citizens residing outside France (permanent residents of France are ineligible); any discipline relating to Mediterranean studies; English sufficient (French desirable). 1 or 2 grants awarded. PROVIDED: €2,400/month stipend; fully equipped apartment with linens; office in co-working space; visa fees waived; academic event organisation expenses covered; school placement assistance for dependents. DURATION: 5 or 10 months (September 2027 – June 2028, flexible). DEADLINE: 15 September 2026. Apply via the Fulbright Scholar Program at https://fulbrightscholars.org/award/fulbright-imera-mediterranean-studies-award-aix-marseille-university.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Pioneer Works: 2027 Open Call – Visual Arts and Music Residency (Brooklyn, NY)
item.description:
Organisation: Pioneer Works

Location: Pioneer Works, Red Hook, Brooklyn, New York, USA

Award: Visual Arts: USD 5,000 one-time award (USD 2,500 honorarium + USD 500/month stipend for 5 months). Music: USD 3,000 one-time award (USD 2,500 honorarium + USD 500/month stipend for 1 month). Free studio space and facilities access. No housing or travel provided. No application fee.

Duration: Visual Arts: 5 months. Music: 1 month. Cohort begins after jury decisions in late September 2026.

Deadline: 13 Jul 2026

Pioneer Works in Red Hook, Brooklyn invites applications from emerging visual artists and musicians for its 2027 residency cohort. 12 Visual Arts residents (5-month residency) and 12 Music residents (1-month residency) are selected each year through a two-phase jury process. WHAT IS PROVIDED: free studio space; access to facilities including risograph printer, darkroom, ceramics studio, large-format printer, 3D printers, thermobinder, and laser cutter; biweekly studio visits with curators, gallerists, critics, and artists; free access to all public programmes at Pioneer Works, including Second Sundays (monthly open studios and performances). FINANCIAL AWARD: USD 5,000 for Visual Arts residents; USD 3,000 for Music residents (each comprising a USD 2,500 honorarium plus USD 500/month for the residency duration); applicants' choice on how to spend it. Pioneer Works does not provide housing, travel, or visa sponsorship. ELIGIBILITY: open locally, nationally, and internationally; collectives may apply (resources shared); current students and programme alumni are not eligible. REVIEW PROCESS: phase one is internal scoring on technical ability, uniqueness of vision, and future potential (nontraditional career paths given special consideration); highest scorers advance to an external jury of art professionals and alumni who assess diversity of demographics and media, genuine studio need, and fit with Pioneer Works' public-facing culture. Jury convenes September 2026; all applicants notified by late September. Supported by Dalio Philanthropies, the Andy Warhol Foundation, NYC Department of Cultural Affairs, New York State Council on the Arts, and the National Endowment for the Arts. APPLY: via Pioneer Works website by 13 July 2026. Questions: residency@pioneerworks.org.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: MAK Center: Artists and Architects in Residence 2027/2028 (MAK Schindler Scholarship, Los Angeles)
item.description:
Organisation: MAK Center for Art and Architecture, Los Angeles

Location: Los Angeles, California, United States (Mackey Apartments). Residents must be present in L.A. for the full six-month term.

Award: Six-month MAK Schindler Scholarship residency with accommodation provided at the Mackey Apartments. The open call does not state a stipend, travel or honorarium figure; confirm any additional financial support directly with the MAK Center. No application fee stated.

Duration: Six months. Residency terms run October to March or April to September; 2027/2028 cycle.

Deadline: 12 Aug 2026

The MAK Center for Art and Architecture awards Artists and Architects in Residence scholarships (the MAK Schindler Scholarship) to independent projects exploring the relation between art and architecture. Projects are chosen for their ability to critically examine current trends in art, architecture and society; to make a compelling connection with Los Angeles as the place of implementing or continuing the applicant's own work; and to demonstrate a conceptual and experimental approach. Emphasis is placed on the L.A. location, and residents' projects often reflect, incorporate and address specific facets of the city and its social fabric. Recipients are encouraged to take part in the MAK Center's activities and to attend meetings, workshops and project discussions with other artists and students. ELIGIBILITY: freelance artists; fine arts or architecture students at a university who have completed the '2. Studienabschnitt'; or architects or artists who have recently graduated. Teams of up to three are eligible if all members practice together as an artist group or collective of architects, are all present in L.A. for the six months, and name a contact responsible for the team. HOUSING: the Mackey Apartments are made available to scholarship holders only; family members, relatives and friends may be accommodated for a maximum of two weeks during the term, permitted in advance by Beth Stryker, Director of the MAK Center. Eligibility is excluded if the applicant has been or will be granted another scholarship in the USA immediately before or after the MAK scholarship, or if the data form is incomplete or the required portfolio is missing. Residency terms run October to March and April to September. Deadline 12 August 2026. Apply: https://www.makcenter.org/apply-mackey

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Frame Fellowship 2.0 (Residential Accelerator for AI Safety Content Creators) 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Frame Fellowship

Location: San Francisco, USA; residential (in person).

Award: USD 10,000 stipend for the 10 weeks, plus accommodation, meals and flights covered. Access to production-ready studios in San Francisco, a mentor and creator network, and connections to funding, sponsorship and partnership opportunities at organizations such as FLI, CAIS and Seismic. Top performers may be eligible for additional grant opportunities beyond the program. No application fee.

Duration: 10-week residential program

Deadline: 10 Jul 2026

Frame Fellowship is a 10-week residential accelerator in San Francisco for content creators educating the public on AI safety. Frame's premise is that content creators can solve a long-standing bottleneck of expanding reach and trust beyond the AI safety and effective altruism (EA) bubble; creators today hack attention, empower mass action and shape culture. Past Frame Fellows include award-winning filmmakers, journalists from leading newsrooms and ex-activists who transitioned to educating, empowering and preparing their communities for the impacts of AGI. Frame helps amplify their impact to millions of people, connects them to funding and sponsorship opportunities at organizations such as FLI, CAIS and Seismic, and helps establish their path as a leading content creator. This cohort runs 10 weeks with a stronger mentor and creator network, access to production-ready studios in San Francisco, housing, food and a stipend, plus connections to funding and partnership opportunities after the program. TWO TRACKS: (1) Independent Track, build your own channel and become a defining voice on a topic; (2) Amplifier Track, embed with an AI org/company and grow their reach. SUPPORT: Frame covers accommodation, meals, flights and a USD 10,000 stipend for the 10 weeks so fellows can go all in; top performers may be eligible for additional grant opportunities beyond the program. Early deadline: 10 July 2026. Apply at https://framefellowship.com/.

Open call details

item.title: Upstream Creative Writers / Grassroots Journalism Residency 2026: Writing About Natural Places, Stewards, and the Spaces We Care For
item.description:
Organisation: Upstream

Location: Remote residency in Minnesota, USA; applicants must be Minnesota residents living in East Side St Paul; West Central Minnesota (Becker, Clay, Douglas, Grant, Otter Tail, Pope, Stevens, Traverse, and Wilkin Counties); or the Iron Range (Aitkin, Carlton, Cook, Itasca, Koochiching, Lake, and Saint Louis Counties).

Award: USD 3,000 artist stipend, plus mileage reimbursement up to USD 500, a Minnesota State Parks Pass, mentorship and creative support, and the opportunity to publish and share work statewide. No application fee.

Duration: 9-month remote residency

Deadline: 06 Jul 2026

The Upstream Creative Writers / Grassroots Journalism Residency invites creative writers, poets, storytellers and grassroots journalists to spend intentional time over nine months exploring Minnesota's parks, waterways, forests and natural places, and the people who care for them. The residency seeks writers who care about storytelling rooted in place, environment, community, stewardship and connection. SELECTED MINNESOTA ARTISTS RECEIVE: a USD 3,000 artist stipend; mileage reimbursement up to USD 500; a Minnesota State Parks Pass; mentorship and creative support; and the opportunity to publish and share their work statewide. ELIGIBILITY: applicants must be a Minnesota resident, 18 years or older, with a residence in East Side St Paul; West Central Minnesota (Becker, Clay, Douglas, Grant, Otter Tail, Pope, Stevens, Traverse, and Wilkin Counties); or the Iron Range, Minnesota (Aitkin, Carlton, Cook, Itasca, Koochiching, Lake, and Saint Louis Counties). Applications open 8 June 2026; deadline 6 July 2026 by 11:59pm. Apply via the online JotForm.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Harvard Radcliffe Institute: Fellowship Program 2027-2028
item.description:
Organisation: Harvard Radcliffe Institute

Location: Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA; fellows must relocate to and reside in the Cambridge/Boston area for the full fellowship (September through May).

Award: USD 78,000 stipend plus an additional USD 5,000 for project expenses. Relocation, housing, and childcare funds may also be provided. No application fee.

Duration: One academic year, September 2027 through May 2028 (residential)

Deadline: 10 Sep 2026

The Harvard Radcliffe Institute Fellowship offers scientists, writers, scholars, public intellectuals, and artists a year to pursue ambitious projects in a vibrant interdisciplinary setting amid the resources of Harvard University. The Institute welcomes innovative work that confronts pressing social, scientific, and policy issues and seeks to engage audiences beyond academia. Reflecting Radcliffe's history, it welcomes (but does not limit eligibility to) proposals focused on women, gender, and society, or that draw on the Schlesinger Library's collections. It also invites proposals relevant to the Institute's 2024-2029 focus area, academic freedom and connecting across difference (intellectual virtues, free and open inquiry, diversity of thought, political polarization, peace and conflict, inequality, religious pluralism, and related higher-education policy issues), including work that constructively challenges disciplinary orthodoxies or advances transformative perspectives. SUPPORT: USD 78,000 stipend plus USD 5,000 for project expenses, with relocation, housing, and childcare funds available; fellows must reside in the Cambridge/Boston area from September through May. ELIGIBILITY: open to individuals across career stages; tenure is not required and applicants need not be academics. This is NOT a postdoctoral fellowship; those currently enrolled in a degree program are ineligible, as are former Harvard Radcliffe fellows (1999-present). Applicants must meet discipline-specific criteria. APPLY: register on the online portal and select an area (Humanities and Social Sciences; Creative Arts; Nonfiction and Journalism; or Science, Engineering, and Mathematics). Materials: application form, CV, 1,400-word project proposal (with bibliography when appropriate), a writing or work sample, and three references. DEADLINES: humanities, social sciences, creative arts, and nonfiction and journalism by 10 September 2026, 5pm ET; science, engineering, and mathematics by 1 October 2026, 5pm ET.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Civic Science Postdoctoral Fellow: Science Policy Collaborative (George Mason University) 2026
item.description:
Organisation: George Mason University (with UC Riverside), via the Science Policy Collaborative and Civic Science Fellows network

Location: Fairfax, Virginia, USA (hybrid eligible).

Award: Salary approximately USD 80,000 per year plus benefits. No application fee.

Duration: 18-month postdoctoral appointment (two contracts: 12 months + 6 months)

Deadline: 05 Jul 2026

A grant-funded, full-time Civic Science Postdoctoral Fellowship supporting the Science Policy Collaborative, a national network that strengthens the development, sustainability and evaluation of U.S. science policy programs. The fellow helps shape an emerging field at the heart of a growing community of practice connecting science policy programs across the country. WHAT YOU'LL DO: conduct a national needs assessment of U.S. science policy programs and publish the findings; support the Collaborative's working groups by scoping deliverables and building partnerships to execute them; develop infrastructure for knowledge exchange (tools like logic models and evaluation frameworks); connect programs with national and international experts on the use of research evidence (URE) and evidence-informed policymaking; present at partner meetings and convenings; and receive mentorship from George Mason University, UC Riverside and other leaders of the Collaborative. WHO THEY'RE LOOKING FOR: someone with a doctoral degree, relevant experience, knowledge of science-for-policy programs, and skills in network coordination, needs assessment or program evaluation, and writing for broad audiences (see the posting for exact criteria). Fairfax, VA (hybrid eligible); full-time 18-month postdoctoral appointment issued as two contracts (12 months plus 6 months); salary about USD 80,000/year plus benefits. APPLY with a cover letter, CV, three references and a writing sample written for broad audiences; for full consideration apply by 5 July 2026 (open until filled). Questions to search coordinator Natalie V Lapidot-Croitoru, nlapidot@gmu.edu.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Bolder Futures Fellowship 2026: AI for Social Good (sponsored by Micron)
item.description:
Organisation: Bolder Futures (sponsored by Micron)

Location: United States; fully remote. Stipends can only be issued to individuals with US-based tax status (SSN or ITIN); US-based nonprofits with employees elsewhere may be considered. Sessions run during 9am-5pm PT.

Award: USD 2,000 stipend on successful completion (the program notes a comparable paid upskilling program is valued at USD 7,000 or more). No application fee.

Duration: 12-week program, September to December 2026; roughly 8-10 hours per week

Deadline: 30 Jun 2026

A 12-week fall fellowship that bridges emerging AI technology and the social sector: fellows gain practical, workplace-ready AI skills through hands-on, interactive training while applying them to increase the operational capacity of a partner nonprofit. The program emphasises moving past theoretical AI concepts into immediate, real-world applications, and provides equitable access to AI training for individuals and organizations driving positive social change. WHO SHOULD APPLY: professionals and emerging leaders seeking practical AI skills; prior AI experience is not required. ELIGIBILITY: applicants must be at least 18, secure a commitment from a 501(c)(3) nonprofit partner, and commit roughly 8-10 hours per week. Fully remote (sessions during 9am-5pm PT); stipends can only be issued to individuals with US-based tax status (SSN or ITIN), though US-based nonprofits with employees elsewhere may be considered. COMPENSATION: USD 2,000 stipend upon successful completion. The program runs September to December 2026. DEADLINE: 30 June 2026, 11:59pm PT.

Open call details

item.title: CALI Futures 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Center for Cultural Innovation (CCI)

Location: California, USA. Applicants must reside full-time in California

Award: USD 5,000 project-restricted grants, awarded competitively. USD 100,000 total available each year across the 2026-2028 funding cycle. No application fee.

Duration: Grant support period December 2026 to December 2027

Deadline: 28 Aug 2026

CALI Futures supports artists and cultural workers across California, individually and in teams, who are meaningfully contributing to alternative efforts outside of conventional nonprofit and for-profit arts and culture systems. The Fund encourages work focused on income, ownership, and care, and uplifts the role of artists and cultural workers in shaping and sustaining alternative efforts that provide greater financial stability, strengthen creative ownership, and deepen mutual support across the broader arts ecosystem. Project-restricted grants of USD 5,000 are awarded through a competitive process. Competitive applicants present (a) a clear project request; (b) proof of an active, current artistic or cultural practice; (c) a description of their contribution to an alternative effort that improves financial sustainability, creative ownership, or mutual support and addresses challenges not sufficiently solved by conventional nonprofit or for-profit sectors; and (d) framing that ambitiously describes the larger implications of this work for transforming artists' lives and future cultural possibilities. The Fund supports individuals and teams at every stage, from initial ideas to research, implementation, experimentation, and reflection. ELIGIBILITY: applicants must be individual artists or cultural workers (cultural producers, culture bearers, creatives, cultural practitioners); must reside full-time in California; must be contributing to an alternative effort improving financial sustainability, creative ownership, or mutualistic social support within the arts and culture sector; and that contribution must have occurred or begun on or after 1 January 2020. Teams are eligible, but all team members must meet the criteria and only one application is accepted per team (one member applies and is responsible for grant requirements). INELIGIBLE: individuals without an active practice; organizations seeking operating or program support; arts administrators; individuals living or working outside California; past CALI Catalyst grantees describing the same project funded in 2021-2025; incomplete applications; and those with a conflict of interest with CCI or Hewlett Foundation board, staff, or directors. The Fund is administered by the Center for Cultural Innovation (CCI) with funding from the William and Flora Hewlett Foundation. TIMELINE: applications open 9 June 2026 at 9:00 a.m. PT; deadline 28 August 2026 at 11:59 a.m. PT; review September to November 2026; notifications by 17 November 2026. Submitted via Submittable. Questions: grants@cciarts.org (include CALI FUTURES in the subject line) or 415.288.0530.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: United We Om: Karma Project Grants (Fall 2026)
item.description:
Organisation: United We Om

Location: United States. Applicants and their service work must be based in the USA

Award: Micro-grants of USD 500 to 2,500 (line-item budget required). Service compensation capped at USD 50 per hour.

Duration: Grants must be completed within six months of receiving funding; awarded September 2026

Deadline: 30 Jun 2026

United We Om's Karma Projects micro-grants support and amplify the good works of people dedicated to karma yoga, acts of selfless service without attachment to outcomes, that create lasting change for individuals, communities, and humanity. The Fund seeks to support individuals and small nonprofits creating change in their communities, using their own life experiences to serve others facing similar struggles, where USD 2,500 would significantly benefit the work and represents 1% or more of an annual budget (nonprofits with annual budgets of USD 250,000 or less). Preference is given to those performing service work without financial compensation. The Fund supports fully formed ideas and projects with specific goals and outcomes: expansions of existing programs, innovative pilots, and projects providing necessary resources to people without access, that can be sustained beyond the grant period without further United We Om funding. NOT FUNDED: projects that are not karma yoga; wellness services to communities with traditional access; inflated or above-market budgets; art, film, music, or theatre performances that do not directly serve an underserved community; organizations with annual budgets over USD 250,000; programs requiring membership or ongoing participant fees; one-day events; partially funded projects (additional funds must already be in place); applicants or service work outside the USA; for-profit businesses; projects led by people not from the community they serve; projects with any participation fee including by-donation; religious activities (mantra and prayer); therapy or medical/herbal advice without an active license; political or societal activism; and projects still in development. Grant funds may not be used for staff salaries, travel, administrative or grant-prep work, legal or accounting services, volunteer stipends, tuition or fee subsidies, software/website/tech, or attendance incentives. Grantees may pay themselves up to USD 50 per hour for active service time. APPLICATIONS: two windows annually. The Fall 2026 window is open and closes 30 June 2026 at midnight; grants awarded September 2026. Separate applications exist for individuals and for nonprofits. Do not use AI to write the application. Questions: Executive Director Matt Jared at matt@unitedweom.org.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Van Alen Institute: Open Access - Open Call for Exhibition Proposals 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Van Alen Institute

Location: Brooklyn, New York, USA. Exhibition at Van Alen Institute, 28 September to 13 November 2026

Award: USD 3,000 honorarium, plus archival access, curatorial support, mentorship, free materials via Materials for the Arts, professional documentation, and inclusion in the public exhibition. Five projects selected. No application fee.

Duration: Hybrid project check-ins July to November 2026; exhibition 28 September to 13 November 2026

Deadline: 28 Jun 2026

Open Access: Exploring 130 Years of American Design is a request for exhibition proposals that treats the Van Alen Institute archive (thousands of competition boards, jury records, photographs and correspondence) as living material to be questioned and reinterpreted through the lens of open and fair access. Van Alen invites emerging designers and creatives of all disciplines to engage the archive, either responding directly to specific materials (competition prompts, submitted drawings) or thematically. A wide range of media is welcomed, including architectural models, drawings, images, projections, video, photography and writing; direct engagement, interaction and multi-disciplinary collaboration are encouraged. Five projects will be selected and shown together at Van Alen Institute, 28 September to 13 November 2026. ELIGIBILITY: open to emerging creatives across disciplines (architects, artists, designers, filmmakers, photographers, researchers, writers and others); no formal architectural training required. Applicants must be at least 18; be legally authorized to work in the United States OR capable of receiving an artist honorarium (so non-US applicants able to receive an honorarium may apply, though the Brooklyn exhibition and hybrid check-ins make it US-practical); be available for hybrid check-ins July to November 2026; and commit to delivering exhibition-ready work on the project timeline. Collaborative proposals are permitted, though the honorarium may be shared among collaborators. Selected participants receive a USD 3,000 honorarium, archival access and curatorial support, mentorship from Van Alen staff and advisors, free materials through Materials for the Arts, and professional documentation. Supported by the National Endowment for the Arts and the New York State Council on the Arts. Application deadline: 28 June 2026.

Open call details

item.title: ZwillGen Fellowship Program 2027-2028 (Technology Law)
item.description:
Organisation: ZwillGen

Location: Washington, D.C., USA (must work out of the DC office)

Award: Salary USD 115,000 to USD 125,000, with a reduced annual billable target (1,250 hours) to create space for writing, professional development, and policy engagement. No application fee.

Duration: 12-month position beginning Fall 2027

Deadline: 31 Aug 2026

The ZwillGen Fellowship is a 12-month position for recent law-school graduates and early-career attorneys interested in working at the intersection of law, technology, and public policy. Fellows work alongside attorneys on matters involving privacy, cybersecurity, artificial intelligence, online platforms, surveillance, digital advertising, fintech, online safety, and technology regulation, contributing to client counseling, investigations, litigation, and emerging regulatory matters, as well as research, writing, and speaking. The program includes a reduced billable target (1,250 hours) to make room for thought leadership and engagement with the technology policy, privacy, and civil liberties community. ELIGIBILITY: must have graduated from law school before the program starts; must have taken or plan to take a bar exam near the start of the Fellowship; must be able to work out of the DC office and be eligible to apply for admission to the DC Bar. Candidates primarily interested in traditional IP practice may find it less aligned. A small number of applicants may also be considered for the Hannah Schaller Memorial Skylark Fellowship (privacy-law focus, with a reduced 1,100-hour billable target for additional community engagement). Following the Fellowship, fellows may be invited into a full-time attorney role or supported in pursuing other technology law and policy opportunities. APPLY with a resume, law-school transcript, writing sample, and cover letter by 31 August 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Exploratorium: Artist-in-Residence Program (AIR) 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Exploratorium

Location: San Francisco, California, USA (embedded within the museum)

Award: USD 15,000 annual stipend, plus travel support, project management and financial support for residency projects, and access to Exploratorium facilities and staff expertise. No application fee.

Duration: Typically two years (exploratory and project-development phases)

Deadline: 29 Jun 2026

The Exploratorium's Artist-in-Residence Program (AIR), running since 1974, works with individuals and artist groups who are drawn to collaboration, interested in interdisciplinary dialogue, and open to developing new working methods. Projects have taken many forms: multimedia performances, theatrical productions, animated filmmaking, immersive installations, walking tours, and online projects. The program lets artists embed within the culture of the institution (a renowned San Francisco science museum), with access to its staff and a diverse public for cross-pollination. Residencies typically unfold over two years and include both an exploratory phase and a project-development phase. The program is designed for artists who begin with curiosity and experimentation rather than a fully formed idea, working co-creatively with the Exploratorium; applicants should be inherently curious and deeply invested in inquiry as part of their practice. SUPPORT: a USD 15,000 annual stipend, travel support, project management and financial support for residency projects, and access to Exploratorium facilities and staff expertise. Apply via the Exploratorium SlideRoom portal by 29 June 2026.

Open call details

item.title: Tech, Media & AI Policy Fellow (CMDG, Open Markets Institute)
item.description:
Organisation: Center for Media & Digital Governance, Open Markets Institute

Location: Washington, D.C., USA

Award: USD 20 to 25 per hour. Candidates who bring external funding or support that offsets compensation are encouraged to note it; total compensation may be structured accordingly.

Duration: Fellowship (hourly)

Deadline: 30 Jun 2026

A fellowship at the Center for Media & Digital Governance (CMDG) at the Open Markets Institute, a Washington-based think tank, for early-career professionals interested in how AI is reshaping the information ecosystem, how platform and media consolidation affect press freedom and democratic accountability, and what regulatory and legal tools can address concentrated power over the news. The fellow contributes to research, publications, regulatory comments, op-eds and testimony; supports communications across the Center's Substack, social media and partner outreach; and shares administrative work (scheduling, convening logistics, organizational systems). QUALIFICATIONS: strong research and writing skills; a bachelor's degree or higher in journalism, law, policy, communications or a related field (law, policy, or graduate background and prior publication or journalism experience preferred); comfort with social media and AI tools; experience with data analysis, regulatory agencies, and/or policy tracking; international or non-U.S. regulatory exposure a plus. TO APPLY: send a resume, one-page cover letter, and one writing sample to jobs@openmarketsinstitute.org with the subject line 'CMDG Fellowship Application'; links to relevant social media or published work welcome; no phone calls. NOTE: applications are reviewed on a rolling basis, so there is no firm deadline; the date shown is an approximate cutoff and applying earlier is advised.

Open call details

item.title: Sight/Geist Season 7: Film & Performance Open Call for Emerging NYC Artists
item.description:
Organisation: Shelley & Donald Rubin Foundation (The 8th Floor)

Location: The 8th Floor, near Union Square, New York City, United States

Award: Fixed artist fees: $300 for a group screening; $500 for a solo screening; $750 for a solo performance, with an additional $250 for reimbursable production costs (collaborators, materials, transportation, rehearsal, specific AV/lighting beyond the gallery inventory). For duos and collectives the fees are shared. The primary applicant must be able to submit an invoice and US tax form to receive payment. No application fee.

Duration: 2026/27 programming season at The 8th Floor

Deadline: 05 Jul 2026

Seventh season of Sight/Geist, a series from the Shelley & Donald Rubin Foundation supporting emerging NYC-based film and performance artists. The Foundation provides curatorial, administrative and promotional support, plus documentation for performances and discussions; selected artists also engage in a Q&A on their broader practice. TWO CATEGORIES: (1) single-channel screening - experimental/non-commercial cinema, video art or performance documentation as a single-channel non-looped projection with 2.1 PA; H.264 or ProRes; under 80 minutes (priority to <20 min); (2) performance and expanded forms - 20-50 minutes; may foreground duration, movement, audiovisual media, voice, site specificity, audience participation; conceptual and political proposals encouraged. ELIGIBILITY: primary applicant must be at least 18 and maintain their primary residence in New York City (or spend most of their artistic, professional and social life in NYC); self-identify as an 'emerging' artist (early-career, self-trained, newly graduated, or currently enrolled in undergraduate/graduate programs); one submission per artist/duo/collective. Submissions open 1 June through 11 pm ET, 5 July 2026; applicants notified by late August 2026. Contact: info@the8thfloor.org.

item.title: PMRCAA Residency 2027: Process and Material (Pine Meadow Ranch)
item.description:
Organisation: Pine Meadow Ranch Center for Arts & Agriculture / The Roundhouse Foundation

Location: Pine Meadow Ranch, Sisters, Central Oregon, United States

Award: Free residency (no application or residency fee). Stipend of $200 per week to offset living and travel expenses; private studio + room in the shared Hammond House. Travel reimbursement up to $300 for qualifying residents based more than 1,000 miles one-way from the ranch (limited number; trustee-approved; paid post-residency). Stipend is conditional on completing the full residency session.

Duration: 2-week or 4-week residencies between March and mid-November 2027; cohorts of 2-4 residents share the Hammond House

Deadline: 06 Jul 2026

Residency on a working 260-acre ranch in Sisters, Central Oregon, near Bend and Redmond, for artists, ecological scientists and scholars exploring connections to nature, land conservation, historic preservation, agriculture and community building. THEME 2027: Process and Material - investigating the fundamentals of practice on a working ranch; materials as memory and meaning; cross-disciplinary work welcome including ceramics, photography, textiles, arborology and beyond. STUDIOS: 9 studio spaces adapted to different mediums (Tent Cabin, Kiln Room, Pickle Room, Old Shop, Tack Room, two Hammond House studios, Cooper's Penthouse with flatbed scanner/inkjet/GlowForge laser cutter; Studio 6000 off-site for printmaking; the Dairy Barn is unavailable in 2027). 24/7 access; private studio per resident plus shared Hammond House (private room with shared/private bathroom, kitchen, dining, laundry). Wi-Fi in main spaces and select studios. The property is NOT ADA-accessible (historic site). Residents contribute via a community-engagement element (workshop, artist talk, or Studio Tour for the public; participation in Studio Tour is required). ELIGIBILITY: US-based applicants only; emerging and established artists/scientists; no specific educational qualifications; collaborations submit one joint application. Alumni may re-apply every two years. COVID-19 vaccination required (or medical exemption discussed in advance); pets not allowed; ranch equipment and farm machinery off-limits without permission. TIMELINE: application opens 1 May 2026 (Slideroom); virtual info session 15 May; application closes 6 July 2026 at midnight PST; finalist interviews 12-14 August; references due 24 August; decisions announced September 2026. Selected by panel of external reviewers active in PMRCAA's mission areas.

Open call details

item.title: WeHo Artist Grant 2027 (City of West Hollywood)
item.description:
Organisation: City of West Hollywood, Arts Division

Location: West Hollywood, California, United States

Award: $6,000 per artist (total category pool of $30,000/year, so approximately 5 awards). Funds may be used in any capacity to fulfil the proposed project within the calendar year. No application fee.

Duration: Calendar-year project; mandatory public presentation at WeHo City Hall in November 2027 plus a 3-5 minute film summarising the project

Deadline: 01 Jul 2026

Direct-to-individual artist grant from the City of West Hollywood aimed at nurturing the long-term development of an artist's practice: realising work, advancing conditions of creation, and navigating the complexities of making art and making a career. The City's goal is to keep artists in West Hollywood, attract new artists, and contribute to the city's economic and social well-being. Awardees produce a 3-5 minute film describing the project and its contribution to quality of life in WeHo, and present publicly at City Hall in November 2027. ELIGIBILITY (strict): legal address must be in the City of West Hollywood (no exceptions; proof of residency may be required); applicants must be registered on the West Hollywood Artists Registry; previous WeHo Artist Grant recipients are NOT eligible; students cannot apply in their discipline of study; cannot also be funded by another City Division or Department or co-sponsored by a Council office for the same project; only one grant category per artist per calendar year; City elected/appointed officials, employees and immediate family ineligible. PROJECTS NOT FUNDED: fundraisers, capital campaigns, murals, or religious-based programmes/events. REVIEW CRITERIA (40 points): vision and clarity of project (10), impact of funding on the applicant and WeHo community (10), portfolio (10), professional resume (10); peer-review panel forwards recommendations to the Arts and Cultural Affairs Commission. TIMELINE: deadline 3pm Wed 1 July 2026; panel reviews August 2026; ACAC approves September 2026; notifications and mandatory orientation November 2026; earliest award of funds March 2027 (subject to contracting compliance). Submit two work samples (news clips do not count) plus a clear creative process and budget.

item.title: Grand Canyon Conservancy Residency 2027 (Artist / Astronomer / Environmental Educator)
item.description:
Organisation: Grand Canyon Conservancy (Grand Canyon National Park)

Location: Grand Canyon National Park, Arizona, United States

Award: Free on-site accommodation in the park, financial support (honorarium/stipend; amount varies by track and session - confirm on the track-specific page), plus marketing/exposure through the Conservancy and the National Park. Alumni network access. No application fee. Three tracks (Artist / Astronomer / Environmental Educator) - apply to one track per cycle.

Duration: Place-based residency at Grand Canyon National Park during 2027; dates and length vary by track; residents are expected to engage the public through programs, workshops, talks or similar

Deadline: 15 Jul 2026

Grand Canyon Conservancy's award-winning residency program invites artists, scientists, historians and educators to live on site at Grand Canyon National Park, pursue place-based research, and engage the public through meaningful programs that deepen understanding of the park's environmental, spiritual and cultural impact. Founded in 2020; competitive application + peer-panel review. THREE TRACKS: (1) Artist in Residence - for contemporary artists making interactive (immersive and/or participatory) work that shapes how people experience place; (2) Astronomer in Residence - for astronomers from any discipline who wish to share their knowledge and enthusiasm for dark skies; (3) Environmental Educator in Residence - for educators who use unique and inspiring methods to pique curiosity, prompt exploration, and build knowledge of the world. Residents receive free accommodation, financial support, and marketing/exposure; alumni return as part of a nationwide network. Read the individual track pages (linked from the main residency page) for track-specific eligibility, session lengths, stipend amounts, public-engagement expectations and any region/citizenship requirements before applying. APPLICATIONS OPEN: 15 May - 15 July 2026 for the 2027 cycle.

item.title: Lanesboro Arts BIPOC Artist Residency 2026-2027
item.description:
Organisation: Lanesboro Arts

Location: Lanesboro, Minnesota, United States

Award: Weekly stipend of $1,250 for food, mileage and other costs while in residence (half paid two weeks before residency, half on the final day). Additional budget up to $500 per residency for art supplies if a creative project is pursued. Free private housing (St. Mane Theatre apartment or Art Loft above the gallery) plus access to studio space, kitchen, laundry, Wi-Fi, and an optional Lanesboro Liaison for community/outdoor-recreation connections. No application fee.

Duration: 1 or 2 weeks; residencies take place between September 2026 and April 2027; 8 total weeks distributed across 6-12 residents

Deadline: 10 Aug 2026

Residency for Minnesota-based BIPOC artists (Native American/American Indian/Native Alaskan, Asian, Black/African American, Hispanic/Latino/Chicano/Latinx, Middle Eastern/North African, Native Hawaiian/Pacific Islander, SWANA) and their families, run by Lanesboro Arts in rural southeastern MN. NO OUTPUT OR PRODUCT REQUIRED: the residency provides whatever the artist needs (restoration, family getaway, connection to nature, access to rural community, artistic practice) within the context of the community; only one community share-back event is required (workshop, school visit, small gathering, or blog post; arranged with Lanesboro Arts staff after selection). HOUSING: private bedroom + bath in either the St. Mane Theatre Artist Residency Center (sleeps 1-4) or the Art Loft above the Gallery (sleeps 1-5); kitchenette/full kitchen, studio space, laundry, Wi-Fi; neither space is currently ADA-accessible. COHORT OPTION: solo, OR apply with a specific BIPOC artist collaborator, OR apply open to being matched with another BIPOC resident at the same time (both receive the stipend; lodging split between St. Mane and the Art Loft). Spouses/partners only get a stipend if they themselves are applying as artists in the cohort model. SELECTION: lottery process (no traditional artistic-merit gatekeeping); 6-12 residents chosen depending on session lengths; lottery is recorded and shared with applicants for transparency. ELIGIBILITY: BIPOC artists currently based in Minnesota. APPLY via the Lanesboro Arts website. TIMELINE: initial deadline 11:59pm Monday 10 August 2026; lottery the week of 10 August 2026; rolling applications thereafter if slots remain, until 1 April 2027 or until full. Lanesboro Liaison (staff or vetted community member) optionally available for tours, grocery delivery, and outdoor recreation (biking, kayaking, hiking, state parks, etc.).

Open call details

item.title: Centre for Curatorial Leadership: Fellowship 2027 for Art Museum Curators
item.description:
Organisation: Centre for Curatorial Leadership (CCL) (with Columbia Business School faculty)

Location: Two weeks in New York City, USA (11-22 January 2027); a five-day residency at a host museum; concluding week in May or June 2027

Award: Tuition free. CCL covers the majority of travel, hotel and food costs for the programme: for the two weeks in NYC and the final week, the majority of meals and Mon-Fri transportation are organised by CCL (Fellows cover a small number of taxi/incidental costs); for the five-day individual residency, CCL arranges and pays for hotel and travel, and Fellows receive a daily stipend for meals, transportation and incidentals. No application fee.

Duration: January to May or June 2027, in three blocks: 2-week NYC intensive (11-22 Jan 2027); 5-day individual residency at a host museum (Feb-Apr 2027); concluding week (May or June 2027)

Deadline: 30 Jun 2026

The Centre for Curatorial Leadership (CCL) offers leadership training for art museum curators across all art-historical specialties. The core Fellowship provides experienced curators with instruction from Columbia Business School faculty and exposure to real-world challenges faced by cultural institutions today. Mentoring is a key element: directors and trustees from major museums across the world host Fellows for a weeklong residency. PROGRAMME STRUCTURE: (1) a two-week intensive in New York City (11-22 January 2027) with Columbia Business School faculty teaching plus practical exposure and assignments; (2) a five-day individual residency in February-April with a museum director from an institution other than the Fellow's home institution; (3) a concluding week in May or June crafted to the particular needs of the class. ELIGIBILITY: full-time senior and/or established curators working in art museums in North America and abroad; up to 12 applicants accepted each year. DEADLINE: 30 June 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Light Work: Artist-in-Residence 2027
item.description:

Location: Syracuse, New York, United States

Award: $7,500 stipend plus furnished housing and 24-hour access to Light Work's digital lab, studios and equipment. No application fee stated; check before applying.

Duration: One-month residency (one of several months across 2027)

Deadline: 01 Jul 2026

One-month artist-in-residence programme at Light Work in Syracuse, New York, for artists working in photography or image-based media. Residents receive a $7,500 stipend, furnished apartment-style accommodation, 24-hour access to Light Work's lab and equipment, and editorial/curatorial engagement (publication, exhibition, archival inclusion). ELIGIBILITY: open to artists from any country working in photography or image-based media; current students are not eligible. Submit via SlideRoom. Applications for the 2027 cycle are open through 1 July 2026.

Open call details

Location: United States (in-person seminars in Los Angeles, New York and Israel)

Award: $10,000 stipend plus all travel and accommodation for three in-person seminars (Los Angeles, New York and Israel). No application fee.

Duration: 2026-27 lab (three in-person seminars)

Deadline: 15 Jul 2026

Screenwriters Lab from the Jewish Writers Institute, pairing a cash stipend with three fully-covered in-person seminars to develop screenwriters' craft and projects. ELIGIBILITY: US-based screenwriters aged 21 or older who meet at least one industry credential (WGA membership, representation, completion of a recognised program, an optioned script, or writers'-room experience). item.title: Centrum: 2027 Residency Application (Self-Directed, Emerging Artist, In the Making)
item.description:
Organisation: Centrum

Location: Fort Worden, Port Townsend, Washington, United States

Award: Residency offering dedicated time, workspace and accommodation at Fort Worden. Financial terms (any stipend or cost to the artist) are not specified at this application stage. No application fee.

Duration: 2027 residency season (dates vary by program)

Deadline: 30 Jun 2026

Centrum's single application covers three 2027 residency programs at Fort Worden in Port Townsend, Washington: Self-Directed Residencies (approx. 55-60 spots), In the Making Residencies (3-5 spots), and the Emerging Artist Residency (6 spots). The program gives artists, writers and creatives across all disciplines time and space to relax, focus and reinvent their practice, alongside a community of other residents. Open to practices including but not limited to visual arts, writing, curatorial, performance, dance, music and social practice. Applicants submit work samples/portfolio, the workspace/accommodation they need, and a short paragraph on what a residency would mean to them, and indicate which program(s) they want to be considered for. Selection is by a panel of alumni jurors; no feedback is offered. ELIGIBILITY: Self-Directed and In the Making are open broadly; the Emerging Artist Residency is restricted to Pacific Northwest-based artists residing in Washington, Oregon, Idaho, Montana or British Columbia. Application window 15 April - 30 June 2026; applications absolutely cannot be accepted after 30 June 2026, so apply early.

Open call details

item.title: Light Work Artist-in-Residence 2027 (Syracuse, NY)
item.description:
Organisation: Light Work

Location: Syracuse, New York, USA (in-person, one-month residency on site)

Award: $7,500 stipend plus a furnished apartment and access to state-of-the-art photography facilities. The residency concludes with a feature in a special edition of 'Contact Sheet: The Light Work Annual' alongside a commissioned essay. No application fee.

Duration: One-month residency in 2027

Deadline: 01 Jul 2026

Light Work, an independent non-profit founded in 1973, offers a long-running artist-in-residence programme dedicated to the development of experimental and contemporary photographic practices. Over 400 artists have participated in the residency, with many going on to gain international recognition. ELIGIBILITY: artists working in photography or image-based media, from any country and at any career stage. Apply via the Light Work SlideRoom portal.

Open call details

item.title: Rowland Fellowship at Harvard (incoming 2027 cohort)
item.description:
Organisation: The Rowland Institute at Harvard University

Location: Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA (Harvard main campus)

Award: Salary from $89,999 per year with full Harvard benefits; yearly operating budget from $225,000 for lab supplies, travel and hiring personnel (postdoctoral fellows, postbacs, undergraduates); generous start-up funding for capital equipment based on the research programme; dedicated laboratory space plus ancillary spaces (e.g. tissue culture); full principal investigator rights; staff scientist and engineer support for designing and fabricating experimental setups; shared research equipment across Harvard (Center for Nanoscale Systems, Bauer Life Science Core Facility); mentoring on lab culture, scientific writing, budgeting and leadership; and access to Harvard's Core for Mentorship Excellence. No application fee.

Duration: Up to five years, with flexible start (incoming 2027 fellows start July to December 2027)

Deadline: 01 Aug 2026

Rowland Fellowship at Harvard for outstanding early-career experimentalists in any field of science or engineering, providing the opportunity to establish an independent research programme at the Rowland Institute in Cambridge, Massachusetts. Founded by Edwin Land in 1980 to foster high-risk creative research and joined to Harvard in 2002, the Institute particularly supports scholars with potential to establish ground-breaking research programmes that bridge traditional disciplinary boundaries. ELIGIBILITY: applicants should currently be completing their PhDs or have received their PhD after 1 May 2025; the doctoral degree must be completed prior to starting the Fellowship. Fellows may have the opportunity to teach undergraduates during their Fellowship. Apply via the Harvard Careers posting linked from the Rowland Institute fellowships page. Applications are currently open; the call does not state a fixed closing date, so check the Harvard Careers posting for the current deadline.

Open call details
